speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 13, 1995. The survivor is Mary Stolar. I'm Jackie Lew. The interview will be conducted in English in New York, in Brooklyn, USA. Today is July 13, 1995. I'm Jackie Lew, and the survivor I'm interviewing is Mrs. Mary Stolar. The interview will be conducted in Brooklyn, New York, USA, and it will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mary-- Mary Stolar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell the name you were given at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name, which is Aranowcz. A-R-A-N-O-W-C-Z. Aranowcz. This is the way, in Poland, it's spelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name at birth is Mera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, in Poland, M-E-R-A. It's a little anglicized, and it's M-A-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1919 in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let it be the first of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rovno, R-O-V-N-O, East Poland-- eastern Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Itzhak. My mother's name was Channa Bella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Cemachowicz, C-E-M-A-C-H-O-W-I-C-Z. Cemachowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were they originally from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother comes from Kovno. This is the capital of [? Litvia-- ?] Lithuania. Kovno, the capital of Lithuania. Lithuania. My father comes from Vilna, which is-- used to be [? Litvia-- ?] but it was-- it's Poland. Vilna. V-O-- Excuse me. V-I-L-N-A, Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got-- We were five children. There was three girls and two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tevya, Moyshe, [? Chaya, ?] Risie, and [? Mera. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the fourth, the fourth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am I supposed to be 20-- supposed to be 75, but I'm 76 actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any grandparents or anybody that lived in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was in [? Litvia-- ?] it's such a long story. I-- it was-- I remember my grandmother, which is the mother of my mother, like through a fog, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That she was kind of blind, almost blind, and we have-- when she walked out, want to go someplace, we have to help her out. That was-- I must have been a-- a tiny, little child, but somehow I remember-- it came back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With us. There was a house where my mother lived and her sister. My-- They both married, and they lived in one, shared house, which belongs to the parents. And she was the-- the elderly lady. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your early childhood? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a carpenter, and circumstances-- when I say circumstances, Vilna belonged to-- started to belong to Poland. And my father was in the other side in Vilna, and I was in Kovno with my parents-- with my mother. Later, we immigrated to Vilna. My father was involved with another woman, and this was effect of this whole life, the poorness and the broken home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you move to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I have no dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you remember how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was a very-- very young child. I have no dates, no years, no specific dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we immigrate. Well, my-- my father suggested we try another city. We immigrated to Rovno. Oh, I already said I was born in Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were not born there? Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can we make-- can we say that? Can we redo?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When-- then-- we-- I was-- then-- we'll make it as I was born in Rovno, because I was-- we emigrated to Rovno. I was a young girl, but all the things happening-- my marriage, my son was born, and everything-- then I, consider-- in my passport, I consider-- because I wasn't sure where, then I have to make that one city. Then I consider Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what city do you think you were actually born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace-- someplace in, let's say, Lithuania. My father never hesitate, or I don't remember. Someplace in Lithuania, I would say, in Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then you moved to Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: You will fix it, John?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. It will keep going. So you moved to Kovno? Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have to say that we-- we moved to Rovno, and I was born there. Is it OK? I said [? Litvia. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to stick with Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent my teenage years there. I married my husband there. My son was born there. The-- the Holocaust happened there, and my passport-- there's got to be a specific place, and I made up, and I stick with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were a little girl. Where were you living when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Rovno?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living, kind of, with my stepmother, the other woman in my father's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dimenstejn. You see, I remember her, and her last name. It's amazing. I remember her. Dimenstejn.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would spell it, D-I-M-E-N-S-T-E-J-N. Stejn. Dimenstejn.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's different, completely. The alphabet, the Polish-- it's different. And her name is Rachelle. And what else? And then-- you know, I can say-- make a statement, and then you can close it?

speaker: interviewer
text: We can close it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- all right. I was born in Rovno I said, right? Then be moved to a city called Baranowcz. B-A-- It's spelled like my name, only with a 'B' in the beginning. You see? You spell like Aranowcz Baranowcz. Baranowcz. Where-- the reason why I mention it, is this is the place where I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's important, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went-- they put my parents-- I consider my, I mean, stepmother, my father. They were almost illiterate. There many people were illiterate in Poland and Eastern Europe. And Yiddish, in order to know Yiddish, to read a Yiddish book, to write a Yiddish letter, was very important. It was very popular among Jewish people. They placed me in a private, Yiddish school because Yiddish wasn't taught in public school. I attend-- I attended this private school a very short time, unfortunately. Even I picked up Yiddish. I write and read fluently because it was private, and it's got to be paid. We couldn't afford to pay. I was placed in a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of the schools? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Yiddish school was just a Yiddish-- a Yiddish school, a Yiddish shul. The public school was just-- [SPEAKING POLISH]. Public school's [SPEAKING POLISH] in Polish. I attended a few years, maybe four. I was in the fourth grade, and we start there. We moved back to Rovno, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and in Rovno, I started my teenage years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father doing? He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was carpenter?

speaker: interviewer
text: --a carpenter. Both times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was Baranowcz from Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is an-- Baranowcz is what-- is-- is Belarus kind of. It's more Russian-Ukrainian part. Russian part. Rovno is kind of like near the Ukraine. It's kind of the Ukrainian part. It's-- it's-- Poland is not so terribly big. And-- they-- it was different. Baranowcz, I don't know by-- by distance, by kilometers, how very far, but it was not near.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your brothers and sisters together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. That's another story. My-- my-- my rest of-- of my siblings remained with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rovno, I-- I started to look for work. We were very poor. How can I, a teenager, a young-- in my young teens-- I was babysittering. I was, you know. Couldn't possibly do some-- and I didn't have a specific profession. And I started to go out with girls, with boys, and I attended for a short time evening school. I wanted to have a little more education. They called-- they called this the evening [INAUDIBLE]. Also has to be paid a little bit. Everything was paid, and I couldn't afford it. I interrupted it again, but I read-- I read a lot. I was one of the best pupils in school, and I was attending school the best. I was reading, and I am kind of a self-educated person. I-- I was every little print what you can find on the floor, on the street, I would-- I would miss. And I was very interested in-- in-- more in nonfiction, in politics even. I knew everything what's going on. The-- the King of England [INAUDIBLE]-- resigned, gave up the throne, and I was involved in it with the news. And the Reichstag-- when they burned the German Reichstag, and I was so very-- the headlines. I was running to the special printing place. They got these paper-- newspapers in the windows, displayed with the headlines. And I was, here as a little girl, a young teenager, being involved. People sitting, standing, adults reading, and I pushed myself into-- I was very, very-- a very intelligent child, but I couldn't develop this, unfortunately. Nobody was thinking about it. There was no circumstances. And I met my husband. I was about 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chiam. He was blond, blue eyed like my son, very handsome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno. He was a real Rovno-born. Rovno. And we started to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was actually-- he was actually a house painter, but he hated it is another thing. They used to neglect children, sending him to do something because of the [? plane ?] and poor parents. We married in 1938. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] In '38. My son, [? Eric, ?] was born in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was the name you gave him at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lieber. L-I-B-E-R. My-- my husband's sister died recently, and your name was Lieber. They insisted. I didn't like it, but I wasn't probably-- I didn't have any influence.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my son was born in 1939 in February, the 20 of February. [INAUDIBLE] all the details. They war broke out-- the war in a few months. He was a few months old. The war started. In 1939, he was a few months old, and they already started to bomb Rovno. They bombed it. And-- and the pact between the nations-- Germany, Poland Russia-- as you people know, there is a famous fact, historical fact. That's, Poland will be divided. Russia claims a part of it. Germans claims a part of it. And they divided Poland. And we were [INAUDIBLE] to the Russians. The Communists marched, and not that it was so very, very pleasant. It was far from it, but there were-- we already heard about Hitler's philosophy with Jews, and we were on the Russian side. We are far from it. And-- and refugees started to come from central Poland-- the refugees, where Hitler took over the Poland-- the half of Poland come to other-- this other half. And we find out, and we started talking about concentration camps, but we were under the Communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Up until that point had you felt anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, ho, ho. Here, I have the article. I-- here I have the article. I just find it this week in I-- a horrible article about anti-Semitism, and I-- I made-- I sent it to my son in Israel. And when I spoke to him, he said, Mama, we have it here too. And I showed it to my co-workers over there. I made a copy, and I have it here. I'll show it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me about when you first felt the trouble coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely discrimination. Jews were-- I consider Poland worse than Germany. I know it's unusual. It's-- you have no idea. I was kicked around in school. We were discriminated terribly. We were not second-class citizens. We were third-class citizens. I don't know if they don't-- they don't talk about it. Well, they know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to hear your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. Well, I don't look Jewish, as you know. And I would believe that. I look Slavic more. My son, absolutely not-- blond, very blond, and very blue eyed. But in spite of everything, we've got neighbors, school people, and everybody knew I'm Jewish. I mean, you can't hide it. You know, you live in places. You know. They were discriminated. They were kicked around. They were calling us names. We killed the Jesus. Always in Christmas time, the-- Poland is 99%-- 98% Catholic. Always then, before-- shortly-- shortly before Christmas Eve, we have to avoid them, not to go out in the evenings. Many times they were-- little pogroms. Ooh. There was not, in the history of Poland, a policeman, a Jewish policeman, even a mail deliverer. Not in the history of Poland. They were representing the-- the same, the parliament. There was representative, naturally, we are citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one thing-- I'm going to mention it now. It's a very important thing. I want to mention it now. These-- there are books. You heard about Lucy Dawidowicz? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You heard about her? I took the book in the library. I read a lot. And there was-- her story when she went to Poland to find about the Jewish organizations. She wrote-- Anyway, there's a-- Is John Jewish? Because I don't want that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the reason I ask. No, it's just recently. Then there is a part where it's something very new to me. 3 and 1/2 million Jews were in Poland. And Poland was already making up things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? You see, I get-- since-- since I am being-- when I get nervous, I stutter. I lose terms to pronounce, and I happened to be very articulate. I used to be in English language. By the way, I speak six languages. And-- then I lose the-- oh, a pact.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's already-- Poland started to make pact with a certain nation, a certain nation-- Madagascar-- I didn't even know-- to chase out 3 million Jews from your country. Just like that, you chase out all the Jews from America. Born there. Several generations born. I couldn't believe my ears. I-- I mean, I said, Mary, you are not naive. You come from there, from this anti-Semitic-- but to just like that, to chase out your citizens. Then my son came shortly after that, and I said to him, oh, Eric. Among other things, my son speaks fluently Polish [INAUDIBLE]. He has a little sentiment for them. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he said, oh, Mama. This is the word he said to me, and I'm still angry. Oh mama, really. That would be a historic-- what are you talking about mama? That would be a historical fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, [? Eric, ?] it's two eventualities. Or I'm lying to you. Or I'm absolutely made up. Or perhaps, I'm just stupid, and I don't know what I'm reading. Boy, was I lucky to find in the library the book. I was so lucky when he was here because not always you find what you want. I find the book, and I brought the thing. And he was sitting in this chair, reading, and reading, and reading. And I said, you are going to apologize to me. He couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's get back to when you felt the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally felt it in every way. Every-- every way. I got some friends. Shiksas, a gentile girlfriend, which is very unusual. I was a little bit involved with-- my Polish was immaculate because they make fun of your accent. My Polish was without accent. I didn't look Jewish, and I-- I was kind of, a little bit, assimilated with Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they-- and then I've got some nice boy-- not a boyfriend, a friend, a male friend. And he would say, [SPEAKING POLISH]. You know what that means? The hell. [POLISH] is the hell. Almost like, go jump in the lake with-- with-- with male Jews, and little female Jews with us. Kind of compliment for the girl-- for the Jewish girls. And they were-- then, like I said-- any-- always-- and then is another thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish Passover-- they need the blood for the matzos, gentile blood. You wouldn't believe it, how very primitive. They will-- even the child wouldn't come so fast from school, from kindergarten, or child would walk out, and oh, oh! My neighbor next door. This was a little girl, [? Danusha. ?] Her name was [? Danusha. ?] This was all later. I'm not-- I'm mixing things up. It's not necessarily the year when it was. And she was cute, and I liked her. She used to come. She was about two and a half years old, and the-- the family downstairs, Mrs. [? Markaska. ?] Oh, did you see [? Danusha? ?] Did you see [? Danusha? ?] She just walked away, and they were-- ssh-- talking to each that, that-- god, if we don't find her. They thought she-- she was kidnapped for using for Jewish-- for the blood for the matzos. You know the matzos. You [INAUDIBLE]. And they were-- I can tell you so many incidents, but then they took the advantage when Hitler walked in. They were so happy. They already were putting [INAUDIBLE], which is swastikas. Not-- there was no-- Germans didn't occupy the part of our, but they knew the news. They already started to-- how do you call the-- And they script things outside. No. The thing which is written all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Graffiti.

speaker: interviewee
text: Graffiti. Putting [INAUDIBLE] which is swastikas. Oy, Hitler's going to finish you up. Then you don't have to go too far, girls, Jackie, and John, and Rob. There was just out-- when Steven Spielberg went with the crew to Krakow, to Poland, when they were filming They said-- I have the article, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they said, who are you? The Polacks looked in the cameras, and they were so. And they say to Steven Spielberg, oh, you are Jews. You are Jews. You are going to film. Ah. And he explained, we are going to film a movie about a gentleman who saved the Jews, who was helping them. And this man said, this Polack said to Steven Spielberg, what? I could have do-- You see right here the gesture. All of them. You see the gesture? All of them. And a woman-- and Steven Spielberg was, what-- what do you mean by that? And you know Kingsley? What's his name? The British actor? Ben Kingsley? He's not Jewish, and he was there in the crew because he's in the movie. He couldn't believe. He was almost-- dragged this guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a woman came over, and she said-- and also the same thing, a Polish woman, she says, well, we didn't want Hitler to occupy our country, but at least he did the job for us. He finished up those Jews we hate. Here, just now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But let's go-- let's go back there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not going to be in the thing, what I'm telling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It better be.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's there. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people [INAUDIBLE]. They don't-- they wipe, and they can spit further. Because-- the reason why I say that is because I just heard on the radio-- a Jewish man who is in symphony orchestra, American symphony orchestra, a member of a violinist or something. They went to Poland. And he was impressed with the symphony orchestra in Krakow. And he said, oh, but it's such in a bad shape financially. I'm going to help to build them up. They-- we-- and he was fighting, and he was donating-- collecting donations. We are going to build up the orchestra. [INAUDIBLE]. And I was lying in bed and listening to the radio, his statements, and I said, you-- go jump in the lake, you stupid thing. Let me put this-- the donations in some someplace in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's go back to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in Rovno. OK. I experienced nothing like every Jew. We used to go out walking with-- We've got the main street, the main way you go [NON-ENGLISH], you go walking. We got two places. Sidewalks, all right? Every street had two sidewalks. On-- the main street, the main place where people were gonna-- going to-- to have fun, at the shopping center, and there was only one big street. One side was Jewish, and the other side we called it Polish side and Jewish side. But-- it wasn't officially. We just made it up for us to go, and only Jewish people were walking this particular side. Trying to go to the other side, and you can be sure you're going to be kicked around, unless he does not recognize that you're a Jewish girl or Jewish boy. Otherwise, he's gonna go, hey, you Jew, go back on the other side. I can write a book, which I did, about the Polish anti-Semitism. Just recently I read about 150 young men and women, Germans, went to Israel to-- to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but let's go back to you. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goyim, Gentiles, went to Israel to help in the kibbutzim, and helping. Never would a Polish young guy go to help Israel. Are you kidding?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened then? What was the next thing that happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Then in 19-- like I said, we were from '39 to '41 under the Communist regime. '41 and it was too good to be true. In '41 they marched into-- when the pact was done by the politicians-- they marched into the part of Poland where I came from. That [? part ?] includes Rovno. And the hell started. OK, we started to have-- there was posters outsides in German and in Polish. We have to wear arm bands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the arm bands?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got it-- I don't remember those little things. We got that-- they-- they [NON-ENGLISH]. Why do you say [NON-ENGLISH]. They arranged a Jew-- Judenrat. Called Judenrat. 'Juden' is Jewish. 'Rat' is kind of a committee. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were just like-- it was just a joke, really. They didn't have an authority, but at least they-- they-- you can go and ask for something. Maybe they've got the bands. And they were having yellow-- How do you say a hat with a [NON-ENGLISH]? Cap.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cap? With this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. A cap.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cap? Around the cap, they've got a yellow ribbon. It was done. It was-- it was-- this was-- they were identified me this, when they wear those hats. A yellow around, around Just made up what--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who wore those--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat. The men. Of course the men. only men was in the Judenrat, only male.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And make a pact, and then we call this Judenrat, and we-- we call this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And they were Jews, naturally. But very little authority, maybe a little knowledge, a little news. Maybe we were given the bands from them. I can tell you that. I do not remember these [? old ?] little details. We were wearing it. OK. After-- and we didn't-- we were-- we were living in our individual apartments. Start--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You know what, maybe we'll stop here. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll change tapes. OK?
